project_overview.project_name: Sierra Leone Second Financial Inclusion Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To promote a more inclusive and resilient financial sector for individuals and micro, small and medium sized enterprises (MSMEs)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Individuals and MSMEs not financially included; women and women-owned enterprises; persons with disabilities; rural populations; financial institutions; mobile money operators; Bank of Sierra Leone; Ministry of Finance; other public and private institutions

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Sierra Leone is a post-conflict country with ongoing fragility challenges; 42% of chiefdoms lack financial services access points; project specifically targets underserved rural and remote areas

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Adoption and Usage of Transaction Accounts
key_components.description: Support adoption and usage of transaction accounts through: (1.1) expansion of digital payment access points including agent banking and POS terminals in rural areas; (1.2) national payments system infrastructure development including RTGS/ACH upgrades and MFI core banking systems; (1.3) enabling environment through regulatory reforms, BSL capacity building, cybersecurity improvements, and financial literacy campaigns

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Increasing Access to Credit for MSMEs
key_components.description: Facilitate access to affordable credit for MSMEs through: (2.1) subsidized line of credit (US$10 million with US$2 million conditional amount) for MSMEs affected by economic shocks, with TA for innovative products; (2.2) enhanced supervisory capacity for BSL's OFISD and other regulators; (2.3) modern credit reference system covering non-banks

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management, Monitoring and Evaluation
key_components.description: Support institutional capacity building, project coordination, financial management, environmental and social safeguards, M&E mechanisms, communication activities, grievance redress mechanism, and citizen engagement through beneficiary satisfaction surveys
Provisional zero-amount component to allow rapid reallocation of credit proceeds during emergencies such as natural disasters or crises with major adverse economic or social impact

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Bank of Sierra Leone (BSL) through a Project Implementation Unit (PIU) within the Financial Sector Development Unit (FSDU)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee chaired by BSL Governor with representatives from relevant ministries, agencies, and private sector; regular coordination with ongoing Sierra Leone Financial Inclusion Project (SLFIP); coordination with IFC's access to finance program; coordination with World Bank's Macroeconomics, Trade, and Investment Global Practice
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations (November 2020); Sierra Leone National Public Procurement Act 2016; use of World Bank's STEP system; competitive selection methods; prior and post-review procedures; procurement risk rated Substantial with mitigation measures including recruitment of procurement specialist, capacity building, and aggregation of small packages
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: M&E team within BSL PIU; quarterly interim financial reports; semi-annual and annual progress reports; beneficiary satisfaction survey at project midterm; impact evaluation at project end; World Bank supervision missions every 6 months; mid-term review; Implementation Completion and Results Report within 6 months of closing

identified_risks.risk_description: 2023 is an election year and political transition could affect Government priorities; governance risks at state-owned banks with 60% of corporate governance benchmarks not observed
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establish broad-based Steering Committee not dependent on individuals; Government commitment to improve governance through action plan to address governance challenges; support for IMF structural benchmark on corporate governance

identified_risks.risk_description: High inflation (37% in January 2023), currency depreciation (66% in 2022), fiscal deficit nearly 10% of GDP, public debt-to-GDP ratio increased to 94%, banks highly exposed to government debt, limited external buffers
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IMF ECF program with staff-level agreement (May 2023); World Bank support to BSL to assess and monitor sovereign debt risk and develop contingency plans; project support for fiscal discipline; ongoing policy dialogue with IMF and World Bank

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial stability risks from performance of two state-owned banks; shallow financial sector with private sector credit only 6% of GDP; crowding-out by government securities; limited focus on private sector lending
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Banks developing risk-based framework with IMF support; BSL developed National Strategy for Financial Inclusion 2022-2026; project supports BSL's financial inclusion strategy; enhanced supervision of state-owned banks

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: BSL uses paper-based systems; staff lack adequate experience with MFI and NBFI risk management; slow decision-making processes and fragmented coordination within BSL noted in previous project; limited supervisory capacity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Engage same PIU from ongoing SLFIP with gained experience; establish robust governance framework with Steering Committee with direct lines to Deputy Governor and Governor; competitively select LOC management firm for apex function; provide TA to strengthen BSL supervisory capacity; establish dedicated unit for MSME loan coordination

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited procurement capacity of implementing agency; limited knowledge of World Bank Procurement Regulations and STEP; inefficiencies and delays in procurement; insufficient competition; weak complaint redress system
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruit procurement specialist; capacity building through regular procurement clinics and trainings; aggregation of small packages where feasible; sensitization of private sector to bid; biannual reporting on complaints; use of STEP for tracking; prior and post-review procedures

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak coordination of project FM requirements; potential delays in financial reporting; risk of noncompliance with internal controls; delays in processing withdrawals; inadequate FM systems
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruit qualified FM specialist; develop comprehensive Project Implementation Manual with FM procedures; configure SunSystems 6 software; include project in BSL internal audit work plan; establish Audit Committee per PFM Act 2016; appoint external auditors; quarterly IFR reviews; annual FM reviews by World Bank

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of exclusion of vulnerable groups (women, PWDs, elderly, remote communities) from project benefits; potential for elite capture and favoritism in MSME credit selection; risk of SEA/SH; limited accessibility of financial institutions and digital devices for PWDs; gender-based violence risks from women's economic empowerment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Prepare and implement GBV/SEA/SH Action Plan within one month of effectiveness; establish project-specific Grievance Redress Mechanism with multiple uptake locations and SEA/SH procedures; prepare Environmental and Social Management Framework and Labor Management Plan; conduct beneficiary satisfaction survey at midterm; targeted outreach to women and PWDs; accessibility improvements for digital platforms; code of conduct for project personnel

identified_risks.risk_description: Country faces significant risk of cybersecurity disruptions to payment systems infrastructure
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Conduct cybersecurity assessment of payment system infrastructure; implement recommendations from assessment; provide TA and investments for cybersecurity improvements; support ongoing five-year cybersecurity plan; strengthen network resilience and private sector capacity to respond to cyber-attacks

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of coordinating multiple components across BSL departments and with external stakeholders; need for high-level decisions from BSL and MoF management
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust governance structure with Steering Committee; direct lines of communication to decision-makers; lessons learned from previous projects incorporated; dedicated PIU with experienced staff; regular coordination meetings

triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental and Social Standards (ESS) 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 2: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-specific Grievance Redress Mechanism with multiple uptake locations accessible to all stakeholders; includes specific procedures for SEA/SH complaints with multiple accessible entry points, referral to GBV service providers, and confidential survivor-centered complaint management protocols; also accessible through World Bank's Grievance Redress Service and Accountability Mechanism
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Strong gender focus: target of 55% of LOC disbursements to women-led MSMEs and 20% to climate adaptation/mitigation; special outreach to women entrepreneurs; TA to develop innovative credit products not relying on traditional collateral; support for women's financial literacy; monitoring of women credit registry users and women-led MSME loans; recognition that women face higher barriers (collateral, skills, literacy gaps); project will help close gender gaps in financial inclusion (8 percentage point gap in account ownership)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project addresses fragility through: (1) strengthening financial sector resilience to economic shocks; (2) expanding financial inclusion to reduce poverty and inequality which are drivers of conflict; (3) supporting MSMEs to maintain employment and reduce youth unemployment; (4) building institutional capacity of BSL for financial sector stability; (5) promoting digital payments to reduce cash-based transactions and associated security risks; (6) supporting climate adaptation to reduce climate-related shocks that can trigger conflict; (7) inclusive design targeting marginalized groups to reduce exclusion-based grievances
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sierra Leone is a post-conflict country (civil war 1991-2002) facing multiple fragility factors: (1) extreme poverty (56.8% national poverty rate, per capita GDP US$480); (2) high youth unemployment; (3) weak institutions; (4) north-south ethno-regional divide; (5) recent Ebola epidemic (2014-2016) with 4,000+ deaths; (6) macroeconomic instability with high inflation and currency depreciation; (7) low financial inclusion (29% of adults); (8) limited access to finance for MSMEs (44% cite finance as constraint); (9) high exposure to climate disasters (30+ in 25 years); (10) governance weaknesses; (11) limited institutional capacity
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project addresses security through: (1) digitalization of payments reducing need for cash handling and associated security risks; (2) government-to-person payment digitalization improving security of transfers; (3) financial inclusion reducing economic desperation that drives crime; (4) MSME support maintaining employment and reducing youth unemployment; (5) institutional strengthening of BSL improving financial sector stability; (6) cybersecurity improvements protecting payment system infrastructure from attacks
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: No - Project does not involve land acquisition, resettlement, or displacement. Subcomponent 1.1 explicitly states no major civil works will be supported and any rehabilitation or minor works must be within existing physical footprint of existing structures with no change in land use, resettlement, or environmental/social risks. Component 2 LOC excludes subprojects involving resettlement or significant land use changes. However, project may benefit refugees and displaced persons through improved financial inclusion and access to credit if they are in project areas.
